paragraphs.body: Imagine if we stood in the middle of the street and got ran over by a truck tomorrow  Frontal Lobe 8:31pm Raw Feelings Brain group chat B Why are we not writing about Mom?

annotations.comment: I love love this "brain group chat." What a fabulous use of the hermit crab model!

paragraphs.body: Parietal Lobe 7:23 pm Because it makes me sad to think about her because I miss her and what if we write something that hurts her feelings and then she also gets sad and doesn’t love us anymore………. Temporal Lobe 7:51 pm Omfg ok well if we are scrapping the essay about Mom that we literally spent HOURS on? What are we going to write about? Parietal Lobe 8:08 pm dude everyone is tired of your intrusive thoughts, STOP, this does not feel right. Its 8:51pm right now. I’m here, sitting on my dorm bed staring at my computer screen which is blurry because my eyes are filled with tears. To tell you the truth I think this is the most vulnerable I’m going to ever get in my writing, I’m so tired of life and that is utterly terrifying to me, I am a young adult in college, and I am supposed to be happy, but I don’t feel that way. Every time I turn a year older, and I hear Happy Birthday being sung to me I focus on the light of the candles and find myself surprised that I didn’t give into suicide. I heard the sound of someone laughing a few rooms away just now, each chuckle is a reminder of that I don’t have anyone to laugh with here, yes I have friends but it’s different when you feel like you are the scum of the earth and shouldn’t burden other people lives by existing. Society has proven to me that people can chew you up and spit you out like a piece of gum. I have learned to live hating myself, but I don’t know how much longer I can keep it up although I have said things to this degree before so its worth acknowledging that this isn’t the first time I’ve felt this way, nor will it be the last. Outside of attending classes and working on schoolwork you can usually find me scrolling on my phone or sleeping, the three courses I currently take at the University of Pittsburgh now are probably contributing reasons to why I have not done anything more drastic. I am very grateful for the life I do have but I feel like I’m living most of it on autopilot. I just want to feel happy so bad please somebody get me out of this. I promise I am trying my best, but I feel like people don’t believe me, if only they could see a glimpse into my life, I promise it was not easy. When I was 12 I had brain cancer and watched a boy on the ward ask his mother if the doctors were going to amputate his leg, the mom didn’t respond, she couldn’t he knew what this meant, and he kept his composure because there were younger kids in the room who he didn’t want to scare. I’ve met kids on that ward who didn’t make it, I had to grow up with survivors guilt before I became a teenager, and I had to learn how to walk again. When I was a teenager my mom cheated on my dad, they were together for 40+ years and my younger brother became addicted to drugs, I had to become the mother of the family so that I could take of my low functioning twin brother. My younger brother would go on to verbally abuse me, but I was told to not take it personally and that “boys will be boys.” Five months ago a tree fell on top of my dad as he was riding his mountain bike through the woods, so now I come home every weekend to help him take care of my now adult younger brother who refuses to finish high school as well as my twin brother. I had to call 911 this past weekend because of how bad his back pain was. On very rare occasions I hear him mutter something to himself about being better off dead than dealing with the agonizing physical pain he suffers. I only really got to realize how far disconnected I am from myself last Wednesday when my therapist referred me to get a psychiatric evaluation. When I figured out I had been normalizing passive thoughts of suicide ever since I was in sixth grade. The last time I felt truly happy I went into a panic attack because that feeling of joy was so foreign, I felt like there was something medically wrong with me. I will continue to search for this elusive emotion and maybe at some point I won’t have any intrusive thoughts of harming myself. At the end of the day I acknowledge that I did not end up being able to model any of the three essays, but these words are the ones that I didn’t have to spend four hours staring at my computer screen to type. So for me this Is an accomplishment. Now it is 10:35 and I need to take my anti-depressants and cry into my pillow.

annotations.comment: This is heavy, Luna. I'm feeling, strongly, your pain here. Are you okay? Do you have some support?

I had to become the mother of the family so that I could take of my low functioning twin brother.
annotations.comment: This is another possible idea to develop into a whole essay, as well!

annotations.text: When I was 12 I had brain cancer and watched a boy on the ward ask his mother if the doctors were going to amputate his leg, the mom didn’t respond, she couldn’t he knew what this meant, and he kept his composure because there were younger kids in the room who he didn’t want to scare. I’ve met kids on that ward who didn’t make it, I had to grow up with survivors guilt before I became a teenager, and I had to learn how to walk again
annotations.comment: Wow. This is powerful. You could develop this moment into an entire essay.

Hello, What a distinctly humbling experience to be trusted with your ideas, your fears, your anxieties, and your life. I want you to know, first off, that you are an extraordinary human with the most thoughtful, intriguing, and sophisticated view of the world—people, film, literature, etc. etc. etc. You’ve also, clearly, been dealt a very difficult hand, more than anyone—especially someone in their first year of college—should ever have to deal with. My main concern, before we discuss anything else, is that you are safe. You know, of course, to take anyone who speaks of suicide, or hurting themselves or others in any way, seriously. I’m taking you seriously. I’m listening. And I’m asking: do you need help? I can help you find help. Please let me know if this is the purpose of your essay. It may be that I am your audience, then. And I hear you. In terms of the essay itself, the brain group chat is brilliant. What an amazing use of the hermit crab model to introduce the tensions, the internal dialogue, the anxieties that emerge from the details of your life that you introduce later. Your Englishes here are great, too. They speak to the context and broader audience (yourself, other young people suffering with depression and anxiety, others burdened and overwhelmed…) The words that stand out the most to me in this piece are those that address the very concrete and specific details of your life. For example: “I hear Happy Birthday being sung to me I focus on the light of the candles and find myself surprised that I didn’t give into suicide.” Or: “When I was 12 I had brain cancer and watched a boy on the ward ask his mother if the doctors were going to amputate his leg, the mom didn’t respond, she couldn’t he knew what this meant, and he kept his composure because there were younger kids in the room who he didn’t want to scare. I’ve met kids on that ward who didn’t make it, I had to grow up with survivors guilt before I became a teenager, and I had to learn how to walk again.” These moments open the door to much exploration, to discovery, to an essay that only you could write. And, in terms of this project, I think these are the words you needed to write right now. That said, if, in fact, I am not the audience of this essay (as I’ve alluded to above), I’d love to see you develop one idea more fully and completely. You have a whole book of essays embedded in this one project (or, perhaps: a whole series of short films?) There are so many frictional, heat-filled, energetic moments that suggest a potential audience and purpose should they become the center of an essay. Yet, in terms of a broader purpose and audience, how do all of these ideas and experiences, when taken together, “add up” to one center of gravity? And who would you say, specifically, is your audience? You have a voice! You have ideas to share, a powerful worldview. Please keep writing. Keep exploring. Keep finding your people. Thanks and talk to me anytime 😊
